title: practical english usage - Part 117
content:
Metals are mostly shiny.

Specialists are likely to use the for groups or classes that they study or know the Romantic poets (Shelley, Keats, Byron, Wordsworth and a few others) - 19th-century poets belonged to the group) the Impressionists (a particular artistic movement; we  know more or less who - French painters (a large indefinite group) with a relatively definite, limited number of members. Compare: We are more likely to use the if we  are talking about a ‘ closed’  group or class 2 French      painters;        the    Impressionists The Australians suffered heavy losses in the First World War.

New Zealanders don't like to be mistaken for Australians.

This often happens when we  talk about nationalities. Compare: The railways are getting more and more unreliable, (our well-known railways) - It's difficult for railways to make a profit, (any railways) professional body) What has this government done for the farmers? (the whole - Farmers often vote Conservative, (individuals vote) The stars are really bright tonight, (the whole of our familiar night sky) - Stars vary greatly in size, (every one is different) The nurses have never gone on strike, (the well-known professional body) - Nurses mostly work very hard, (nearly every individual) more likely to use the. Compare: we  talk about the group as a whole - as if it was a well-known unit - we  are When  we  generalise about members of a group, we  usually use no article. But if 1  groups:     nurses       or  the    nurses;      railways         or  the    railways?

notes may help.

same idea can be expressed both with the and with no article. The  following correct. The  grammatical distinctions in this area are not very clear; often the both these meanings come  together, and it is difficult to know which  form is reader knows which  people or things we  are talking about (►   136). Sometimes uncountable and plural words (►   140); but we  use the to show that the listener/ we  usually use no article. For example, we  use no article to generalise with When  we  generalise about members of a group, meaning ‘ any/every individual’ , 141 the^difficult        cases the;   my,    your,          this,    that,           Section Determiners:       a/an   and           etc;        etc         12 grammar •  142   special rules and exceptions to/at/from work to/at sea to/in/from town to/in/into/out of bed/prison  to/in/into/out of hospital (BrE) to/at/in/from school/university/college  to/at/in/into/from church Examples are: normally countable nouns are treated as uncountables, without articles.

In some common fixed expressions to do with place, time and movement, 1  common       expressions         without      articles:     in   bed;    at   school 142 special     rule   s  an  d  exceptions       __________ (on the phone) I'm sorry. You've got a/the wrong number.

I usually sit at the side in church. He's the wrong man for me.

The is often used like this with side and wrong.

She kicked him  on the knee.

Jack Perkins is the son of a rich banker, (who may have more than one son) noi (    ... a wked ofra-car.) Lying by the side of the road we saw the wheel of a car.

are several similar possibilities, and it is unnecessary to be more definite.

particular persons or things we  are talking about. This can happen when there We sometimes use the even when it is not exactly clear which  of several 6 She     kicked       him     on   the    knee.;     He  sat    at   the    side.

For similar expressions with no article (e.g. in bed, in hospital), ►     142.1.

I've stopped reading the newspaper because it's too depressing.

Do you sing in the bath?

Most of my friends go to the gym two or three times a week.

1 have some of my best ideas when I'm on the bus.

the to suggest that taking a bus is a common experience that we  all share.

case the bus, for example, does not mean 'one bus that you know about'; we  use thing that are part of everybody's lives, like 'the bus' or 'the hairdresser'. In this We use the (with a singular countable noun) when we  talk about some kinds of 5  on   the    bus;    at   the    gym 'general' meaning (►    140).
